---
title: "Visualizing and presenting the data"
subtitle: "CHC9012 | National Taiwan Normal University"
format: html
---

```{=html}
<style>
  
  /* Tabset Styling */
  .nav-tabs {
    border-bottom: 2px solid #ecf0f1;
    margin-top: 1em;
    margin-bottom: 30px;
  }
.nav-tabs .nav-link {
  font-weight: 700;
  font-size: 1.1em;
  color: #95a5a6;
    border: none;
  padding: 15px 25px;
  transition: all 0.3s ease;
}
.nav-tabs .nav-link:hover {
  color: #2c3e50;
    border: none;
}
.nav-tabs .nav-link.active {
  color: #3498db !important;
    background: none;
  border: none;
  border-bottom: 4px solid #3498db;
}

/* Stunning Headers */
  
  h2 {
    margin-top: 1.8em;
    color: #2980b9;
      font-weight: 600;
  }
h3 {
  margin-top: 1.5em;
  color: #2c3e50;
    font-weight: 600;
}

/* Floating Images with Animations */
  img {
    border-radius: 12px;
    box-shadow: 0 8px 25px rgba(0,0,0,0.06);
    transition: transform 0.4s cubic-bezier(0.175, 0.885, 0.32, 1.275), box-shadow 0.4s ease;
    margin: 2rem auto;
    display: block;
  }
img:hover {
  transform: translateY(-6px) scale(1.02);
  box-shadow: 0 15px 35px rgba(52,152,219,0.2);
}

/* Elegant Gradient Divider */
  hr.rounded {
    border: 0;
    height: 2px;
    background-image: linear-gradient(to right, rgba(0,0,0,0), rgba(52,152,219,0.5), rgba(0,0,0,0));
    margin: 4rem 0;
  }

/* Pro Code Styling */
  code {
    font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace;
    font-size: 0.9em;
    color: #e74c3c;
      background-color: #fdf2f0;
      padding: 3px 6px;
    border-radius: 5px;
  }
pre code {
  color: #333;
    background-color: transparent;
}

/* Clean Tables */
  table {
    width: 100%;
    border-collapse: separate;
    border-spacing: 0;
    border-radius: 12px;
    overflow: hidden;
    box-shadow: 0 5px 15px rgba(0,0,0,0.05);
    margin: 2rem 0;
  }
th {
  background-color: #3498db;
    color: white;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  letter-spacing: 1px;
  padding: 15px;
  font-weight: 600;
}
td {
  background-color: white;
  padding: 15px;
  border-bottom: 1px solid #ecf0f1;
}

/* Emphasized bolding */
  b, strong {
    color: #2c3e50;
      font-weight: 700;
  }
  
  .panel-tabset .nav-tabs, 
  .panel-tabset .nav-pills {
    display: flex !important;
    flex-wrap: nowrap !important;
    overflow-x: auto !important;
    overflow-y: hidden !important;
    -webkit-overflow-scrolling: touch; /* Smooth scrolling on mobile */
    border-bottom: 1px solid #dee2e6; /* Keeps the line consistent */
  }

  /* Optional: Hide the scrollbar for a cleaner look while keeping the functionality */
  .panel-tabset .nav-tabs::-webkit-scrollbar,
  .panel-tabset .nav-pills::-webkit-scrollbar {
    display: none;
  }

  .panel-tabset .nav-item {
    white-space: nowrap; /* Prevents text inside tabs from breaking into two lines */
  }
</style>
```

## Overview

This week, we will learn how to visualize the results

::::: panel-tabset
## 🎯 Goals

**Visualize your results:**

-   What types of figures to select?
-   How to plot multiple information in one figure?

## 💻 Necessary codes and dataset

**A brief summary of this week's new functions:**

-   `ggplot()`: Initializes a ggplot object and creates the blank visual canvas, linking it to the specified dataset.

-   `aes()`: Generates aesthetic mappings, which connect specific columns/variables in the dataset to visual properties of the plot (like the x-axis, y-axis, color, or fill).

-   `geom_boxplot()`: Adds a box-and-whisker plot layer to visualize the distribution, median, and quartiles of continuous data.

-   `labs()`: Modifies labels across the plot, allowing you to customize the main title, subtitle, x-axis text, and y-axis text.

-   `theme_minimal()`: Applies a clean, minimalist design theme by removing the default heavy gray background while keeping faint gridlines.

-   `theme()`: Dynamically customizes deep aesthetic details of the active theme, used here to hide the chart legend (legend.position = "none").

-   `scale_y_continuous()`: Controls the behavior, absolute limits, specific tick intervals (breaks), and out-of-bounds (oob) handling for a continuous y-axis.

-   `1:7`: A shorthand colon operator that generates a sequential integer vector from 1 to 7 (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7).

-   `summarySE()`: A helper function (typically from the Rmisc package) that automatically calculates descriptive summary statistics—including sample size (N), mean, standard deviation (sd), standard error (se), and confidence intervals (ci)—grouped by independent variables.

-   `geom_col()`: Draws a column chart where the heights of the bars represent the actual raw values provided in the y-axis aesthetic mapping.

-   `geom_errorbar()`: Adds vertical error bar elements to the plot using explicit upper (ymax) and lower (ymin) statistical boundaries.

-   `geom_text()`: Draws literal text labels directly onto the plot framework using specified positions and structural adjustments.

-   `round()`: Rounds numerical values to a specified number of decimal places (e.g., round(Value, 2) rounds to 2 decimal places).

-   `geom_jitter()`: Adds a scatter plot layer where data points are randomly shifted by tiny amounts (width and height) to prevent overlap and reveal data density.

-   `seq()`: Generates a highly controlled mathematical sequence of numbers defining a starting point, ending point, and uniform skipping step size (e.g., seq(0, 20000, by = 4000)).

-   `%>%`: The tidyverse pipe operator; passes the output data structure of the left-hand function directly as the first argument into the right-hand function.

-   `distinct()`: Retains only unique/distinct rows from an input dataframe based on specified column values, removing duplicate entries.

-   `select()`: Picks specific columns out of a large dataframe to keep, while dropping all unnamed columns.

-   `factor()`: Converts a standard character or numeric variable into a robust categorical Factor vector, allowing you to lock in custom chronological hierarchies via its levels argument.

-   `c()`: Combine/Concatenate function; groups standalone strings, variables, or elements into a unified vector.

-   `geom_bar()`: Draws a bar chart where the height of the bar is calculated automatically by counting the total number of observations falling into each category on the x-axis.

-   `after_stat()`: A specific ggplot evaluation function that instructs layers to calculate and map internal statistical steps (like count) after the initial data transformation is complete.

-   `scale_fill_brewer()`: Sets categorical color palettes using discrete color configurations originally designed by ColorBrewer.

-   `theme_classic()`: Applies a traditional presentation theme consisting of a pure white background with clean, high-contrast black x and y axis lines.

-   `expansion()`: Configures explicit numerical margins/padding along the outer edges of a scale, preventing text labels or bars from clipping at the boundaries.

<br>

::: {.text-center .mb-3}
<a href="https://github.com/aymeric-collart/CHC9012/blob/main/course-materials/Files/_Internal/CHC9012_PCIbexDataPrepared.RData?raw=true" class="btn btn-outline-primary btn-sm"> <i class="bi bi-download"></i> Download the dataset for the script (it is the same as last week)</a>
:::

<br>

::: {.text-center .mb-3}
<a href="Files/_Internal/DataVisualization.Rmd" download class="btn btn-outline-primary btn-sm"> <i class="bi bi-download"></i> Download the Markdown script </a>
:::

## 📝 Homework

**Please complete the following tasks before our next meeting:**

1.  Please annotate the Markdown file from this week, knit to a PDF file, and upload on Moodle!

2.  **Next week = Assistance for handling results.** Please have the Markdown document(s) to import your results, clean the dataset and explore/visualize the data ready!
:::::

::: {.text-center .mb-4}
<a href="week14_slides.html" class="btn btn-outline-primary" target="_blank"> <i class="bi bi-window-stack"></i> View the slides of this week's tutorial [incomplete for the moment]</a>
:::

<br>

# 👀 Part 1: Why using figures, and what kinds of figures?

::: {.callout-note icon="true"}
## Why plotting the data?

Strictly speaking, we can provide results tables, including all potentially interesting information, and even provide access to the full dataset as well. Then, **why the need to visualize the data?**

-   The nature of human cognition is such that figures are more attractive than tables and texts. It can therefore be very useful to communicate our message more effectively.
-   As we saw last week, summary figures can be very useful to understand the general pattern of results while acknowledging finer-grained variation.
:::

**Introduction to most commons types of figures in experimental linguistics:**

::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills .tabset-fade group="pipeline"}
## 1.1 Box plots for *data distribution*

-   **What it does:** Visualizes the spread, median, quartiles, and outliers of a continuous numerical variable across different categorical groups.

-   **Core question:** How does data distribution compare between groups? (e.g., Which Iris species has the longest petals?)

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Species, y = Petal.Length, fill = Species)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, width = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Pastel1") +
  labs(title = "Petal length distribution by species", y = "Petal length (in cm)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

## 1.2 Bar plots for *categorical counts*

-   **What it does:** Displays counts or frequencies of distinct categories. By default, `geom_bar()` counts the number of rows for each category on the x-axis, hence the need sometimes to summarize the data beforehand.

-   **Core question:** Which categories are the most and least common in our dataset?

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

ggplot(mpg, aes(x = class)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.85, width = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Count of vehicles by class", x = "Vehicle type", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()
```

## 1.3 Line charts for *continuous measurement, generally over time*

-   **What it does:** Connects individual data points chronologically or sequentially to reveal trajectories, spikes, or downward dips.

-   **Core question:** How is our metric moving over a continuous sequence?

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

ggplot(economics, aes(x = date, y = psavert)) +
  geom_line(color = "firebrick", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Personal savings rate trend", x = "Year", y = "Savings rate (in %)") +
  theme_minimal()
```

## 1.4 Scatter plots for *relationships & correlations*

-   **What it does:** Plots individual data points at the intersection of two continuous numeric variables to display positive, negative, or non-linear correlations.

-   **Core question:** Does variable X increase, decrease, or remain unchanged as variable Y changes?

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Sepal.Width, color = Species)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Sepal length vs. Sepal width", x = "Length (in cm)", y = "Width (in cm)") +
  theme_minimal()
```

## 1.5 Combined plots for *more complex data*

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Species, y = Sepal.Length, fill = Species)) +
  # 1. Plot the underlying summary structural distribution
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.4, outlier.shape = NA, width = 0.5) + 
  
  # 2. Layer the raw data points on top (jittered slightly horizontally so they don't stack up)
  geom_jitter(aes(color = Species), width = 0.2, size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Sepal length by species",
    subtitle = "Combining box plots with jittered data points to avoid hiding sample distribution",
    y = "Sepal length (in cm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```
:::

<hr>

# Part 2: A step-by-step guide to plot figures with R

## 2.1 Introduction to `ggplot` and its spirit

::::: grid
::: {.g-col-md-8 .g-col-12}
Most researchers using `R` and even other programming languages often, if not always, use the `ggplot2` package. It gives us full control of what we want to plot, we can customize the figure as we wish by adding legends, annotations, changing the theme and the colors, etc.

`ggplot2` uses a philosophy called the **Grammar of Graphics** (Wilkinson, 2005). The core spirit of `ggplot2` is that **any data visualization can be broken down into a series of independent layers stacked on top of one another.**
:::

::: {.g-col-md-4 .g-col-12 .text-center}
![](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/logo.png){width="140px" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto 15px auto;"}

[Download Cheatsheet (PDF)](https://rstudio.github.io/cheatsheets/data-visualization.pdf){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm role="button" target="_blank"}
:::
:::::

```{r}
#| echo: false
load(file = "CHC9012_PCIbexDataPrepared.RData")
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(Rmisc)
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| include: false


## 2.1 Select the participants with a human-like behavior
PartMeanRT <- aggregate(RT ~ ID_1_TimeReceived,
                        data = data3,
                        FUN = mean)

data_OtherPart <- subset(PartMeanRT, RT > 1000)
data_OtherPart_FullData <- droplevels(subset(data3,
                                             ID_1_TimeReceived %in% data_OtherPart$ID_1_TimeReceived))

plot(data_OtherPart_FullData$RT ~ data_OtherPart_FullData$ID_1_TimeReceived, outline = FALSE)
plot(data_OtherPart_FullData$Value ~ data_OtherPart_FullData$Condition)

## 2.2 Remove outlier trials

### 2.2.1 Absolute values

data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute <- subset(data_OtherPart_FullData, RT > 200 & RT < 20000)

### 2.2.2 Relative values

# Calculate the mean per Participant and Condition
data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute$m_rt <- ave(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute$RT, 
                                         data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute$ID_1_TimeReceived, 
                                         data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute$Condition, 
                                         FUN = mean)

# 2. Calculate the SD per Participant and Condition
data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute$sd_rt <- ave(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute$RT, 
                                          data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute$ID_1_TimeReceived, 
                                          data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute$Condition, 
                                          FUN = sd)

# 3. Subset the data to remove outliers
data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative <- subset(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute, 
                                               RT >= (m_rt - 2.5 * sd_rt) & 
                                                 RT <= (m_rt + 2.5 * sd_rt))

### 2.2.3 Calculate the number of trials removed

FullData_NbTrials <- nrow(data_OtherPart_FullData)
TrimmedData_NbTrials <- nrow(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative)

# Number of trimmed trials
NbTrimmedTrials <- FullData_NbTrials - TrimmedData_NbTrials
NbTrimmedTrials

# Proportion (%)
NbTrimmedTrials*100/FullData_NbTrials
```

-   **Here is an overview of the step-by-step process to create a figure with `ggplot2`:**

:::::::::::::::::::::: {.d-flex .flex-column .align-items-center}
:::::: {style="width: 100%; max-width: 1000px; border: 1px solid #dee2e6; border-radius: 8px; padding: 25px; background-color: #f8f9fa;"}
::::: {.grid .align-items-center}
::: {.g-col-md-5 .g-col-12}
### Step 1: Initialize

Create the empty canvas.
:::

::: {.g-col-md-7 .g-col-12}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4

ggplot(
  data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative
)
```
:::
:::::
::::::

::: {.text-center .text-muted .fs-1 style="margin: 10px 0; font-weight: bold;"}
↓
:::

:::::: {style="width: 100%; max-width: 1000px; border: 1px solid #dee2e6; border-radius: 8px; padding: 25px; background-color: #f8f9fa;"}
::::: {.grid .align-items-center}
::: {.g-col-md-5 .g-col-12}
### Step 2: Indicate what to plot

In `ggplot2` terms, this is calles the 'aesthetics' (`aes()`).
:::

::: {.g-col-md-7 .g-col-12}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4


ggplot(
data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative,
aes(x = Condition, y = Value)
)
```
:::
:::::
::::::

::: {.text-center .text-muted .fs-1 style="margin: 10px 0; font-weight: bold;"}
↓
:::

:::::: {style="width: 100%; max-width: 1000px; border: 1px solid #dee2e6; border-radius: 8px; padding: 25px; background-color: #f8f9fa;"}
::::: {.grid .align-items-center}
::: {.g-col-md-5 .g-col-12}
### Step 3: Indicate how to plot

This is the step where you decide the kind of plot you want to use.
:::

::: {.g-col-md-7 .g-col-12}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4

ggplot(
  data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative,
  aes(x = Condition, y = Value)
) + 
  geom_boxplot()
```
:::
:::::
::::::

::: {.text-center .text-muted .fs-1 style="margin: 10px 0; font-weight: bold;"}
↓
:::

:::::: {style="width: 100%; max-width: 1000px; border: 1px solid #dee2e6; border-radius: 8px; padding: 25px; background-color: #f8f9fa;"}
::::: {.grid .align-items-center}
::: {.g-col-md-5 .g-col-12}
### Step 4: Polish the details

Change the theme, the color, add a title (and subtitle), change the legend, the font style and size, etc.
:::

::: {.g-col-md-7 .g-col-12}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4

ggplot(
data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative,
aes(x = Condition, y = Value, fill = Condition)
) +
geom_boxplot(fill = "skyblue", width = 0.5) +
labs(
title = "Acceptability judgments by Grammaticality",
x = "",
y = "Rating (1-7 Likert Scale)"
) +
theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
theme(legend.position = "none") +
scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0.5, 7.5), breaks = 1:7)
```
:::
:::::
::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::

## 2.2 Example with the acceptability results

::: {.callout-note icon="true"}
## Aim of this section

**We will look at how to use `ggplot2` to plot a boxplot of the acceptability judgment results we cleaned last week.**
:::

::: panel-tabset
## A. Load the libraries

```{r}
#| message: false

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(Rmisc)
```

## B. Process to data cleaning (Same as Week 13)

```{r}

## 2.1 Select the participants with a human-like behavior
PartMeanRT <- aggregate(RT ~ ID_1_TimeReceived,
                        data = data3,
                        FUN = mean)

data_OtherPart <- subset(PartMeanRT, RT > 1000)
data_OtherPart_FullData <- droplevels(subset(data3,
                                             ID_1_TimeReceived %in% data_OtherPart$ID_1_TimeReceived))

plot(data_OtherPart_FullData$RT ~ data_OtherPart_FullData$ID_1_TimeReceived, outline = FALSE)
plot(data_OtherPart_FullData$Value ~ data_OtherPart_FullData$Condition)

## 2.2 Remove outlier trials

### 2.2.1 Absolute values

data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute <- subset(data_OtherPart_FullData, RT > 200 & RT < 20000)

### 2.2.2 Relative values

# Calculate the mean per Participant and Condition
data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute$m_rt <- ave(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute$RT, 
                                         data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute$ID_1_TimeReceived, 
                                         data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute$Condition, 
                                         FUN = mean)

# 2. Calculate the SD per Participant and Condition
data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute$sd_rt <- ave(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute$RT, 
                                          data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute$ID_1_TimeReceived, 
                                          data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute$Condition, 
                                          FUN = sd)

# 3. Subset the data to remove outliers
data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative <- subset(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsolute, 
                                               RT >= (m_rt - 2.5 * sd_rt) & 
                                                 RT <= (m_rt + 2.5 * sd_rt))

### 2.2.3 Calculate the number of trials removed

FullData_NbTrials <- nrow(data_OtherPart_FullData)
TrimmedData_NbTrials <- nrow(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative)

# Number of trimmed trials
NbTrimmedTrials <- FullData_NbTrials - TrimmedData_NbTrials
NbTrimmedTrials

# Proportion (%)
NbTrimmedTrials*100/FullData_NbTrials
```

## C. Create the canvas

```{r}
ggplot(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative)
```

## D. Include what to plot (the "aesthetics")

```{r}
ggplot(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative,
       aes(x = Condition,
           y = Value))
```

## E. Include how to plot

```{r}
ggplot(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative,
       aes(x = Condition,
           y = Value)) + 
  geom_boxplot()
```

## F. Polish the details

```{r}
# Add colors
ggplot(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative,
       aes(x = Condition,
           y = Value,
           fill = Condition)) + 
  geom_boxplot(fill = "skyblue")

# Add title and legend, change the theme
ggplot(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative,
       aes(x = Condition,
           y = Value,
           fill = Condition)) + 
  geom_boxplot(fill = "skyblue",
               width = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Acceptability judgments by Grammaticality",
       x = "",
       y = "Rating (1-7 Likert Scale)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") 


# Control the y axis
ggplot(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative,
       aes(x = Condition,
           y = Value,
           fill = Condition)) + 
  geom_boxplot(fill = "skyblue",
               width = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Acceptability judgments by Grammaticality",
       x = "",
       y = "Rating (1-7 Likert Scale)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0.5, 7.5),
    breaks = 1:7)
```
:::

## 2.3 Visualizing other results: More about acceptability results, reaction time, and background information

### 2.3.1 Other figures for the acceptability ratings

::: panel-tabset
## Bar plots

1.  First, we create a summary table using the `summarySE()` function

```{r}
JudgSummary <- summarySE(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative,
                         measurevar = "Value",
                         groupvars = "Condition")
```

2.  Then, we create the plot using the same step-by-step logic

```{r}
# Create the canvas
ggplot(JudgSummary)

# Include what to plot (the "aesthetics")
ggplot(JudgSummary,
       aes(x = Condition,
           y = Value,
           fill = Condition))

# Include how to plot
ggplot(JudgSummary,
       aes(x = Condition,
           y = Value,
           fill = Condition)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, alpha = 0.9)

# Include error bars
ggplot(JudgSummary,
       aes(x = Condition,
           y = Value,
           fill = Condition)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = Value - ci,
                    ymax = Value + ci), 
                width = 0.15,
                size = 0.7) 

# Include text
ggplot(JudgSummary,
       aes(x = Condition,
           y = Value,
           fill = Condition)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = Value - ci,
                    ymax = Value + ci), 
                width = 0.15,
                size = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Value, 2)), 
            vjust = 4,
            color = "white",
            size = 5,
            fontface = "bold")

# Control the y axis
ggplot(JudgSummary,
       aes(x = Condition,
           y = Value,
           fill = Condition)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = Value - ci,
                    ymax = Value + ci), 
                width = 0.15,
                size = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Value, 2)), 
            vjust = 4,
            color = "white",
            size = 5,
            fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(1, 7),
                     breaks = 1:7,
                     oob = scales::rescale_none)
```

## Complex figures

We know that there are many inter-item variation in this experiment. We can have a more complex figure to visualize all the information at the same time:

-  **Bar plots** for the general pattern,
-  **Scatter plots** to visualize the results from each trial.

We need two types of summary datasets: (1) general pattern, and (2) by-trial results.

```{r}
JudgSummary <- summarySE(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative,
                         measurevar = "Value",
                         groupvars = "Condition")

JudgSummaryItem <- summarySE(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative,
                         measurevar = "Value",
                         groupvars = c("Condition", "ItemNo"))
```

Now we can plot the data. The `geom_col()` maps with the `aes()` related to the overall pattern. The `geom_jitter()` includes a second `aes()` that maps with the dataset including the by-trial data.

```{r}
ggplot(JudgSummary,
       aes(x = Condition,
           y = Value,
           fill = Condition)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = Value - ci,
                    ymax = Value + ci), 
                width = 0.15,
                size = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Value, 2)), 
            vjust = 4,
            color = "white",
            size = 5,
            fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(1, 7),
                     breaks = 1:7,
                     oob = scales::rescale_none) +
  geom_jitter(data = JudgSummaryItem,
              aes(color = Condition),
              width = 0.2,
              height = 0.1,
              alpha = 0.3,
              size = 2.5)
```

It is the plot that we want, but it is not easy to see all the dots (1 dot = 1 trial). We can adjust the shades and colors, and it is done!

```{r}
ggplot(JudgSummary,
       aes(x = Condition,
           y = Value,
           fill = Condition)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = Value - ci,
                    ymax = Value + ci), 
                width = 0.15,
                size = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Value, 2)), 
            vjust = 4,
            color = "black",
            size = 5,
            fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(1, 7),
                     breaks = 1:7,
                     oob = scales::rescale_none) +
  geom_jitter(data = JudgSummaryItem,
              aes(color = Condition),
              width = 0.2,
              height = 0.1,
              alpha = 0.5,
              size = 2.5) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

By doing so, we have a clear overview of the results, and at the same time, it is very easy to see that the Ungrammatical condition includes some outliers.

:::

### 2.3.2 Visualizing the reaction time

::: panel-tabset
## Boxplot

We can start with a boxplot, such as the one we used last week, but prettier.

```{r}
ggplot(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative,
       aes(x = Condition,
           y = RT,
           fill = Condition)) + 
  geom_boxplot(fill = "skyblue",
               width = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "RT by Grammaticality",
       x = "",
       y = "Reaction time (in millisecond)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 20000),
    breaks = seq(0, 20000, by = 4000))
```

## Bar plot

Such as for the acceptability ratings, we need to summarize the data first, and then plot the bar plot.

```{r}
RTSummary <- summarySE(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative,
                       measurevar = "RT",
                       groupvars = "Condition")

ggplot(RTSummary,
       aes(x = Condition,
           y = RT,
           fill = Condition)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = RT - ci,
                    ymax = RT + ci), 
                width = 0.15,
                size = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(RT)), 
            vjust = 8,
            color = "white",
            size = 5,
            fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(4000, 7000),
                     breaks = seq(4000, 7000, by = 1000),
                     oob = scales::rescale_none)
```

## Complex figures for the RT

We are just using the same logic as previously. We have two datasets, mapping onto two different `aes()` to obtain a figure including the general pattern in addition to the by-trial information.

```{r}
RTSummary <- summarySE(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative,
                       measurevar = "RT",
                       groupvars = "Condition")

RTSummaryItem <- summarySE(data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative,
                         measurevar = "RT",
                         groupvars = c("Condition", "ItemNo"))

ggplot(RTSummary,
       aes(x = Condition,
           y = RT,
           fill = Condition)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = RT - ci,
                    ymax = RT + ci), 
                width = 0.15,
                size = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(RT)), 
            vjust = 8,
            color = "black",
            size = 5,
            fontface = "bold") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(3000, 9000),
                     breaks = seq(3000, 9000, by = 2000),
                     oob = scales::rescale_none) +
  geom_jitter(data = RTSummaryItem,
              aes(color = Condition),
              width = 0.2,
              height = 0.1,
              alpha = 0.5,
              size = 2.5) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

:::

### 2.3.3 Visualizing the background information

Our dataset includes more than 20 observations per participants, meaning that the same type of information (e.g., the age and highest degree of Participant 1) appears more than 20 times. Therefore, we need to create a new dataset where "1 row = 1 participant".

```{r}
ParticipantBackground <- data_OtherPart_CleanAbsoluteRelative %>%
  distinct(ID_1_TimeReceived, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  select(ID_1_TimeReceived, UserAge, UserDegree)

ParticipantBackground$UserDegree <- factor(ParticipantBackground$UserDegree, 
  levels = c("High school", "BA", "MA", "Ph.D.") )
```


::: panel-tabset
## Age of the participants

```{r}
ggplot(ParticipantBackground, aes(x = "", y = UserAge)) +
  geom_boxplot(
    width = 0.4, 
    fill = "#4a90e2",
    color = "black",
    alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 14) +
  labs(x = "",
       y = "Age") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

It is easy to see that the majority of the participants are between 20 and 30 years old. There is only one participant who is much older.

## Highest degree of the participants

```{r}
ggplot(ParticipantBackground,
       aes(x = UserDegree,
           fill = UserDegree)) +
  geom_bar(width = 0.5,
           color = "black",
           alpha = 0.8,
           show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(stat = "count",
            aes(label = after_stat(count)),
            vjust = -0.5,
            size = 5) +
  labs(x = "",
       y = "Number of participants") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Pastel1") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 14) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15)))
```


## Plot "age" and "degree" at the same time

```{r}
ggplot(ParticipantBackground,
       aes(x = UserDegree,
           y = UserAge,
           fill = UserDegree)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.1,
              height = 0,
              alpha = 0.5,
              size = 2.5,
              color = "dimgray") +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.4,
               alpha = 0.7,
               outlier.shape = NA) +
  labs(x = "Highest degree",
       y = "Age") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Pastel1") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```


:::



<hr>

::: {.callout-important icon="true"}
## 📝 Assignment for next week

Please complete the following tasks before our next meeting:

1.  Please annotate the Markdown file from this week, knit to a PDF file, and upload on Moodle!

2.  **Next week = Assistance for handling results.** Please have the Markdown document(s) to import your results, clean the dataset and explore/visualize the data ready!
:::
